items.headline: Wall Street Ends Week Lower Amid Geopolitical Tensions and Inflationary Pressures
items.summary: U.S. equities concluded the trading week on Friday, March 14, 2026, with major indices posting losses as investors grappled with escalating geopolitical tensions in the Middle East and persistent concerns over inflation. Rising crude oil prices, climbing above $100 a barrel, significantly contributed to the downbeat sentiment. The financial sector emerged as the worst-performing group for the third consecutive week. In contrast, Bitcoin showcased remarkable resilience, surging past the $72,000 mark.

items.statContext: Down 0.6% on Friday, marking a 1.60% weekly decline.
Fell 0.9% on Friday, contributing to a 1.26% weekly dip.
Declined 0.3% on Friday, resulting in a 1.98% loss for the week.

items.headline: Sable Offshore Corp.
items.summary: Sable Offshore Corp. led the gainers in March 2026, reflecting strong performance.

items.headline: IREN
items.summary: IREN was a top gainer for the week, shrugging off broader market concerns.

items.headline: Hewlett Packard Enterprise
items.summary: Shares rose after the company released its first quarter fiscal 2026 results, exceeding EPS estimates.

items.headline: Adobe
items.summary: Shares tumbled in Friday's premarket despite record Q1 revenue, impacted by CEO transition and cautious guidance.

items.headline: Ulta Beauty
items.summary: Stock fell after the company reported strong fourth-quarter sales but missed profit expectations and provided weaker guidance for FY2026.

items.headline: Wells Fargo
items.summary: Wells Fargo shares extended losses for a seventh consecutive session, contributing to its weekly decline.

items.headline: Energy and Utilities Lead, Financials and Consumer Cyclicals Lag
items.summary: For the week ending March 14, 2026, the energy sector was the best-performing, up 1.88%, followed by utilities, which gained 0.39%. In contrast, financial services were the worst-performing sector, falling 3.37%, with consumer cyclical stocks also experiencing significant declines of 3.07%. Financials have been the weakest group in the S&P 500 sectors year-to-date.

items.headline: Iran Conflict Escalates, Fueling Oil Price Surge and Market Volatility
items.summary: Geopolitical tensions stemming from the ongoing Iran conflict continued to weigh heavily on global markets. Crude oil prices surpassed $100 a barrel, exacerbating inflationary fears. A drone attack on the Dubai International Financial Center on Friday, March 14, caused minor damage, further highlighting the economic ramifications of the conflict in the Middle East.

items.headline: U.S. and China Begin Economic and Trade Talks in Paris
items.summary: Top economic officials from China and the United States commenced a new round of talks in Paris on Sunday, March 15, aimed at addressing economic and trade issues of mutual concern. These discussions are intended to smooth the path for a potential summit between U.S. President Donald Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping later this month.

items.headline: Key Earnings Reports and Fed Announcements Await Next Week
items.summary: Investors are looking ahead to a busy week with several major companies reporting earnings, including Lululemon Athletica ($LULU) and FedEx ($FDX). The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) is also scheduled to make an announcement and release its Summary of Economic Projections on Wednesday, March 18, followed by a press briefing from Fed Chair Jerome Powell. Origin Materials ($ORGN) is set to release its Q4 2025 earnings before market open on Sunday, March 16.

items.headline: S&P 500 Nears Historically Expensive Levels, Shiller P/E at 39.2
items.summary: The S&P 500 is currently trading at historically expensive valuations, with the Shiller price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio, or CAPE ratio, standing at 39.2. This level approaches the highest seen since mid-2000, raising questions for investors about future market trajectory.
